scenario: You c-bet A♠K♣ on Q♦7♠2♣ (flop), one caller. Turn K♥ — you now have top pair top kicker.
question: You improved to TPTK on the turn. Plan?
options: Double barrel for value — you have a value hand worth betting, and the K improved you; build the pot
explanation: Hardin's turn criteria #1: 'we have a value hand worth betting.' You turned TPTK — bet for value. Betting the turn is crucial to building the pot for a larger river bet. Any card that improves your hand to a stronger value hand is a barrel candidate.

scenario: You semi-bluff c-bet J♠T♠ on 9♠6♦2♣ (flop) with a flush draw + overcards, one caller. Turn 8♥ — you pick up an open-ended straight draw too (now OESD + flush draw).
question: Turn gave you a monster combo draw. Barrel?
options: Check — you still have nothing made
Double barrel semi-bluff — you picked up barreling equity (flush + straight draws = ~15 outs) plus fold equity
explanation: Hardin's turn criteria #2: 'we pick up turn barreling equity.' You now have ~15 outs (9 flush + 6 straight, minus overlap) AND fold equity. Combo draws this strong are mandatory double barrels — you have huge equity when called and fold equity when not. The backdoor-becomes-draw turn is the classic barrel card.

scenario: You c-bet K♣Q♦ on 8♠5♦3♥ (flop), one caller. Turn 2♣ (total blank, didn't help you). You have two overcards, no draw. Opponent folds to turn c-bets only 30% of the time.
question: Blank turn, no equity gained, sticky opponent. Barrel again?
options: No — you have no value, no equity gain, and low fold equity (30% fold to turn cbet). Give up / check
explanation: None of Hardin's four barrel criteria are met: no value hand, no barreling equity gained, opponent doesn't fold enough (30%), and no scare card hit your range. Firing a second barrel here is spew. Check and give up — you'll often still see a free showdown or fold the river. Knowing when to STOP barreling is as important as when to start.

scenario: YOUR HAND. You called a 3-bet with K♠T♠ on Q-J-6. Villain bets flop, you call. Turn blank, villain bets again, you call. River blank, villain fires a THIRD barrel (near pot).
question: Villain triple-barrels into you. You have K-high (the gutshot bricked). Action?
options: Fold — a TAG emptying the clip (3 barrels) on Q-J-6-x-x has a value range (QQ+/sets/AK-made) that you never beat with K-high; micro players don't triple-barrel bluff
explanation: Your real hand (−97 BB). When a tight player fires all three streets ('empties the clip') at micro stakes, it's almost never a bluff — their range is strong made hands that beat your busted K-high. You picked up nothing. The whole hand was a lesson in this: each call compounded the leak. Fold to the triple barrel — believe the story their betting tells.
